Tomato___Bacterial_Spot.description: Bacterial Spot is a serious disease caused by the bacterium Xanthomonas campestris pv. vesicatoria, affecting both leaves and fruits of tomato plants. It thrives in warm, wet conditions and spreads rapidly through rain splash, contaminated tools, and infected seeds. Left untreated, it can cause significant defoliation and fruit loss.
symptoms: Small, water-soaked spots on leaves that turn brown with yellow halos
Lesions on fruit appear as raised, scab-like spots that may crack
Leaf spots coalesce causing large necrotic areas and defoliation
Dark, greasy-looking spots on stems and petioles
Premature leaf drop leading to sunscald on exposed fruits
immediate_actions: Remove and destroy all infected plant material immediately
Avoid working in the field when plants are wet to prevent spread
Disinfect all tools with 10% bleach solution or 70% alcohol between uses
Apply copper-based bactericide as soon as symptoms are noticed
chemical_treatment: Apply copper hydroxide (e.g., Kocide 3000) at 1.25–2.0 lb/acre every 7–10 days
Use copper oxychloride sprays during wet weather conditions
Combine copper bactericides with mancozeb for improved efficacy
Apply fixed copper sprays (copper sulfate pentahydrate) at label-recommended rates
organic_treatment: Spray copper-based organic formulations (OMRI-listed copper octanoate) every 5–7 days
Apply Bacillus subtilis-based biocontrol agents (e.g., Serenade) as a preventative
Use neem oil sprays to reduce bacterial populations on leaf surfaces
Apply hydrogen peroxide-based sprays (diluted 3%) as a surface disinfectant
prevention: Use certified disease-free or hot-water-treated seeds (50°C for 25 minutes)
Practice crop rotation of at least 2–3 years with non-solanaceous crops
Use drip irrigation instead of overhead watering to keep foliage dry
Plant resistant or tolerant tomato varieties when available
Maintain adequate plant spacing for good air circulation
Tomato___Bacterial_Spot.recovery_time: 3–5 weeks with consistent copper treatment and favorable dry conditions

Tomato___Early_Blight.description: Early Blight is a very common fungal disease of tomatoes caused by Alternaria solani, typically appearing on older, lower leaves first before progressing upward. It is favored by warm temperatures (24–29°C), high humidity, and alternating wet and dry periods. The disease can cause significant defoliation and reduce fruit quality and yield.
symptoms: Dark brown to black lesions with distinctive concentric rings forming a 'bull's-eye' pattern
Lesions are initially small (3–4 mm) and appear on older, lower leaves first
Yellow chlorotic halo surrounding individual lesions
Affected leaves turn yellow, wither, and drop prematurely
Dark, sunken lesions may also appear on stems and at the stem end of fruit
immediate_actions: Remove and dispose of heavily infected lower leaves immediately
Apply a fungicide (chlorothalonil or mancozeb) as soon as symptoms are detected
Mulch around the base of plants to prevent soil splash onto lower leaves
Improve air circulation by pruning and staking plants
chemical_treatment: Apply chlorothalonil (e.g., Bravo 720) at 1.5 pt/acre every 7–10 days
Use mancozeb-based fungicides (e.g., Dithane M-45) on a 7-day spray schedule
Apply azoxystrobin (e.g., Quadris) or pyraclostrobin for systemic protection
Rotate between different fungicide classes to prevent resistance development
organic_treatment: Apply copper-based fungicides (copper sulfate or copper hydroxide) every 7 days
Use Bacillus subtilis biocontrol sprays (e.g., Serenade Garden) preventatively
Spray neem oil (azadirachtin) solution every 7–14 days as a preventive measure
Apply a baking soda solution (1 tbsp per gallon of water) to alter leaf surface pH
prevention: Practice crop rotation of at least 3 years with non-solanaceous crops
Use disease-resistant tomato varieties where available
Water at the base of plants using drip irrigation to keep foliage dry
Remove and compost plant debris after harvest to reduce inoculum
Maintain balanced soil nutrition, especially adequate phosphorus and potassium
Tomato___Early_Blight.recovery_time: 2–3 weeks with proper treatment and dry weather conditions

Tomato___Late_Blight.description: Late Blight is one of the most devastating diseases of tomatoes and potatoes, caused by the oomycete pathogen Phytophthora infestans — the same organism responsible for the Irish Potato Famine. It spreads extremely rapidly under cool, wet conditions (10–25°C with high humidity) and can destroy an entire crop within days if untreated. The disease spreads through airborne sporangia and infected plant tissue.
symptoms: Large, irregularly shaped, water-soaked lesions on leaves that rapidly turn brown-black
White, cottony sporulation (mycelium) visible on the underside of leaves in humid conditions
Dark brown, greasy lesions on stems that cause plant collapse
Brown, firm, greasy rot on fruit that extends deep into the flesh
Rapid wilting and collapse of entire plants in severe infections
immediate_actions: Remove and destroy all infected plants or plant parts immediately — do not compost
Apply a protectant fungicide (chlorothalonil or mancozeb) immediately across the field
Avoid overhead irrigation and minimize leaf wetness
Alert neighboring farms as the disease spreads rapidly via airborne spores
chemical_treatment: Apply mancozeb (e.g., Dithane M-45) as a protectant at 5-7 day intervals
Use systemic fungicides such as metalaxyl + mancozeb (e.g., Ridomil Gold MZ) for curative action
Apply cymoxanil + mancozeb (e.g., Curzate M) for both protective and curative activity
Use fluopicolide (e.g., Presidio) or mandipropamid (e.g., Revus) in rotation to prevent resistance
organic_treatment: Apply copper-based sprays (copper hydroxide or copper sulfate) every 5–7 days
Use Bacillus amyloliquefaciens-based biocontrol (e.g., Double Nickel) as a preventive
Apply phosphorous acid (potassium phosphite) to boost plant systemic resistance
Remove infected tissue promptly and maintain strict field sanitation
prevention: Plant certified disease-free transplants and use resistant varieties (e.g., Defiant, Mountain Merit)
Avoid overhead irrigation; use drip irrigation to keep foliage dry
Ensure excellent air circulation through proper spacing and staking
Monitor weather forecasts and apply fungicides prophylactically before high-risk periods
Practice strict crop rotation and remove all volunteer solanaceous plants
Tomato___Late_Blight.recovery_time: Disease progression can be halted in 1–2 weeks with aggressive treatment, but severely infected plants may not recover

Tomato___Leaf_Mold.description: Leaf Mold is a fungal disease caused by Passalora fulva that primarily affects tomatoes grown in humid, enclosed environments such as greenhouses and high-tunnels. It thrives at relative humidity above 85% and temperatures between 22–25°C. While it rarely kills plants outright, severe infections cause significant defoliation and reduce photosynthetic capacity and yield.
symptoms: Pale greenish-yellow spots on the upper surface of leaves
Olive-green to brown velvety mold growth on the underside of leaves corresponding to upper spots
Infected leaves curl, wither, and drop prematurely
In severe cases, blossoms and fruit can also be affected with dark, leathery rot
Disease progresses from lower, older leaves upward through the canopy
immediate_actions: Immediately reduce greenhouse humidity by improving ventilation and reducing watering
Remove and dispose of heavily infected leaves
Apply a fungicide spray to slow disease spread
Increase plant spacing to improve airflow within the canopy
chemical_treatment: Apply chlorothalonil (e.g., Bravo) every 7–10 days as a protectant
Use azoxystrobin or trifloxystrobin (strobilurin-class) fungicides for systemic control
Apply difenoconazole or tebuconazole (triazole-class) for curative action
Rotate fungicide classes to prevent resistance buildup
organic_treatment: Apply copper-based fungicides (copper sulfate or copper hydroxide) every 7 days
Use potassium bicarbonate sprays to create an alkaline leaf surface unfavorable to the fungus
Apply neem oil (1–2% solution) every 7–14 days as a preventive measure
Use Trichoderma-based biocontrol agents to suppress fungal growth
prevention: Maintain relative humidity below 85% in greenhouses through ventilation and heating
Use resistant tomato varieties (many modern greenhouse varieties carry Cf resistance genes)
Avoid overhead watering; use drip irrigation or sub-irrigation
Ensure adequate plant spacing and remove lower leaves to improve air circulation
Sanitize greenhouses and remove plant debris thoroughly between crops
Tomato___Leaf_Mold.recovery_time: 2–4 weeks with improved environmental control and fungicide treatment

Tomato___Septoria_Leaf_Spot.description: Septoria Leaf Spot is one of the most destructive fungal diseases of tomato foliage, caused by Septoria lycopersici. It commonly appears after the first fruit set and spreads rapidly during periods of warm, wet weather. The disease progresses from lower leaves upward and can cause severe defoliation, exposing fruit to sunscald and reducing overall yield.
symptoms: Numerous small, circular spots (3–6 mm) with dark brown margins and grey-white centers on leaves
Tiny dark brown to black pycnidia (fruiting bodies) visible in the center of spots with a hand lens
Yellowing of surrounding leaf tissue leading to leaf death and drop
Disease starts on older, lower leaves and moves upward through the canopy
Severe defoliation leaving plants with only a few leaves at the top
immediate_actions: Remove and destroy heavily infected lower leaves immediately
Apply a fungicide (chlorothalonil or mancozeb) at the first sign of disease
Apply organic mulch around plants to prevent soil splash spreading spores
Stake or cage plants to keep foliage off the ground
chemical_treatment: Apply chlorothalonil (e.g., Bravo 720) every 7–10 days as a protectant fungicide
Use mancozeb (e.g., Dithane M-45) on a weekly schedule during wet weather
Apply azoxystrobin or pyraclostrobin (strobilurins) for systemic and protective activity
Use propiconazole or tebuconazole (triazoles) for curative treatment of active infections
organic_treatment: Apply copper sulfate or copper hydroxide sprays every 7 days
Use Bacillus subtilis-based biocontrol (e.g., Serenade) as a preventive spray
Apply neem oil solution (2%) every 7–10 days to suppress spore germination
Use potassium bicarbonate sprays to inhibit fungal development on leaf surfaces
prevention: Practice a 3-year crop rotation with non-solanaceous crops
Remove all plant debris at the end of the season to eliminate overwintering inoculum
Use drip irrigation and avoid wetting foliage during watering
Maintain proper plant spacing and stake plants to improve air circulation
Apply mulch to prevent soil splash carrying spores onto lower leaves
Tomato___Septoria_Leaf_Spot.recovery_time: 3–4 weeks with consistent fungicide applications and dry weather

Tomato___Spider_Mites Two-spotted_spider.description: The Two-Spotted Spider Mite (Tetranychus urticae) is a tiny arachnid pest (not an insect) that feeds on plant cells by piercing leaf tissue and extracting contents, leading to a characteristic stippled, bronze appearance. Populations explode rapidly in hot, dry conditions and can cause severe damage within days. Heavy infestations produce visible webbing on the undersides of leaves and between stems.
symptoms: Fine stippling or bronzing on the upper leaf surface due to cell damage
Yellowing, browning, and premature drop of leaves in severe infestations
Fine silky webbing visible on the undersides of leaves and between stems
Tiny (0.3–0.5 mm) moving dots visible on the underside of leaves (the mites themselves)
Plant stunting and reduced fruit set in severe, prolonged infestations
immediate_actions: Spray plants forcefully with a strong jet of water to dislodge mites from leaves
Apply a miticide (acaricide) or insecticidal soap spray immediately
Remove heavily infested leaves and dispose of them away from the garden
Increase irrigation to raise humidity, as spider mites thrive in dry conditions
chemical_treatment: Apply abamectin (e.g., Agri-Mek) as an effective miticide, following label directions
Use bifenazate (e.g., Acramite) for rapid knockdown of mite populations
Apply spiromesifen (e.g., Oberon) which affects both adults and eggs
Rotate between different miticide classes to prevent resistance development
organic_treatment: Apply insecticidal soap spray (potassium salts of fatty acids) directly on mites and undersides of leaves
Use neem oil (azadirachtin) solution (2%) every 5–7 days to disrupt mite life cycle
Release predatory mites (Phytoseiulus persimilis or Neoseiulus californicus) as biological control
Apply rosemary oil or peppermint oil-based sprays as natural repellents
prevention: Monitor plants regularly, especially the undersides of leaves, for early detection
Maintain adequate soil moisture and avoid drought stress which favors mite outbreaks
Avoid excessive use of broad-spectrum insecticides that kill natural predators
Introduce and conserve natural predators such as predatory mites and ladybugs
Remove weeds around the garden that can serve as alternative mite host plants
Tomato___Spider_Mites Two-spotted_spider.recovery_time: 1–3 weeks with effective miticide treatment and improved plant care conditions

Tomato___Target_Spot.description: Target Spot is a fungal disease caused by Corynespora cassiicola that affects tomato leaves, stems, and fruit. It is particularly problematic in tropical and subtropical regions with warm temperatures (25–30°C) and high humidity. The disease can cause significant defoliation and fruit blemishes, leading to reduced marketability and yield losses.
symptoms: Circular to irregular brown lesions with concentric ring patterns ('target' or 'bull's-eye' appearance)
Lesions may have a yellow halo and can enlarge to 1 cm or more in diameter
Dark brown, sunken spots on fruit that may be confused with Early Blight
Leaf spots coalesce under heavy infection, leading to large necrotic areas and defoliation
Stem lesions appear as dark, elongated cankers that can girdle and kill affected stems
immediate_actions: Remove and destroy infected leaves and plant debris immediately
Apply a broad-spectrum fungicide promptly upon symptom observation
Improve ventilation and reduce canopy humidity by pruning lower leaves
Avoid overhead irrigation to minimize leaf wetness duration
chemical_treatment: Apply azoxystrobin (e.g., Quadris) every 7–14 days for systemic protection
Use chlorothalonil (e.g., Bravo) as a protective spray every 7 days in wet conditions
Apply fluxapyroxad + pyraclostrobin (e.g., Priaxor) for broad-spectrum fungal control
Use boscalid + pyraclostrobin (e.g., Pristine) for curative and protective activity
organic_treatment: Apply copper-based fungicides (copper hydroxide) every 7 days as a protectant
Use Bacillus subtilis (e.g., Serenade) biocontrol sprays preventatively
Apply neem oil (2%) every 7–10 days to suppress fungal sporulation
Use Trichoderma harzianum-based biofungicides to compete with the pathogen
prevention: Practice crop rotation of at least 2–3 years with non-host crops
Plant in well-drained soils and avoid fields with a history of the disease
Use resistant tomato varieties where commercially available
Stake plants and remove lower leaves to improve air circulation within the canopy
Apply mulch to reduce soil splash and eliminate plant debris after harvest
Tomato___Target_Spot.recovery_time: 3–4 weeks with proper fungicide management and favorable dry conditions

Tomato___Tomato_Yellow_Leaf_Curl_Virus.description: Tomato Yellow Leaf Curl Virus (TYLCV) is a devastating viral disease transmitted exclusively by the silverleaf whitefly (Bemisia tabaci). It is one of the most economically important tomato diseases worldwide, particularly in tropical and subtropical regions. Plants infected early in their growth may suffer near-total yield loss, and there is no cure once a plant is infected.
symptoms: Upward and inward curling of young leaves (cupping), giving a spoon-like appearance
Yellowing (chlorosis) of leaf margins and interveinal areas on young leaves
Stunted plant growth with shortened internodes, resulting in a compact, bushy appearance
Reduced flower set and flower drop, leading to severely diminished fruit production
Older leaves may remain relatively normal while new growth is severely affected
immediate_actions: Remove and destroy infected plants immediately to reduce the virus reservoir
Apply insecticides to control whitefly populations and prevent further spread
Install reflective mulches and yellow sticky traps to deter and monitor whiteflies
Establish insect-proof netting around young seedlings and transplants
chemical_treatment: Apply imidacloprid (e.g., Admire Pro) as a soil drench or foliar spray to control whiteflies
Use thiamethoxam (e.g., Actara) or acetamiprid for systemic whitefly control
Apply spirotetramat (e.g., Movento) for control of whitefly nymphs and adults
Rotate insecticide classes to prevent whitefly resistance development
organic_treatment: Apply insecticidal soap or neem oil sprays every 5–7 days to reduce whitefly populations
Use reflective silver or aluminum mulches to repel whiteflies from young plants
Release natural enemies such as Encarsia formosa (parasitic wasp) for biological whitefly control
Apply kaolin clay sprays to create a physical barrier on plant surfaces
prevention: Plant TYLCV-resistant or tolerant tomato varieties (e.g., Shanty, TY hybrids)
Use insect-proof nets (50-mesh screens) in seedling nurseries and transplant areas
Control whitefly populations aggressively before and after transplanting
Eradicate weed hosts of both the virus and the whitefly vector around fields
Implement a crop-free period between seasons to break the disease and vector cycle
Tomato___Tomato_Yellow_Leaf_Curl_Virus.recovery_time: No recovery — infected plants should be removed; prevent spread by controlling whitefly vectors

Tomato___Tomato_mosaic_virus.description: Tomato Mosaic Virus (ToMV) is a highly stable and persistent plant virus belonging to the Tobamovirus genus. Unlike many other plant viruses, it does not require an insect vector and spreads primarily through mechanical contact — via contaminated hands, tools, clothing, and infected plant debris. The virus can survive for years in dried plant material and soil, making it extremely difficult to eradicate once established.
symptoms: Mosaic pattern of light and dark green (or yellow and green) patches on leaves
Leaf distortion, curling, and blistering with areas of raised dark green tissue
Stunted plant growth and reduced vigor, particularly in young plants
Fruit may show internal browning, uneven ripening, or a mottled external appearance
Necrotic streaking on stems and petioles in some strains (Tomato Streak)
immediate_actions: Remove and destroy confirmed infected plants immediately
Wash hands thoroughly with soap and water before and after handling plants
Disinfect all tools with 10% trisodium phosphate (TSP) solution or 1.5% sodium hypochlorite
Do not use tobacco products near plants, as tobacco can carry related tobamoviruses
chemical_treatment: There are no effective chemical cures for viral infections in plants
Apply insecticides only if aphids or other vectors are also present as secondary pests
Use mineral oil sprays to reduce mechanical virus transmission during plant manipulation
Focus management on prevention and sanitation rather than chemical control
organic_treatment: Apply diluted skim milk spray (1 part milk to 9 parts water) to tools and hands to inactivate the virus
Use neem oil or insecticidal soap to control any secondary insect pests
Maintain plant vigor with balanced organic fertilization to slow symptom progression
Remove infected plant material and deep-bury or burn rather than composting
prevention: Use certified virus-free seeds or treat seeds with trisodium phosphate (TSP) solution
Plant resistant tomato varieties carrying the Tm-2 or Tm-2² resistance genes
Practice strict hygiene — wash hands and disinfect tools between plants and rows
Eliminate solanaceous weeds that can serve as virus reservoirs
Avoid replanting tomatoes in fields with a known history of ToMV without thorough soil preparation
Tomato___Tomato_mosaic_virus.recovery_time: No recovery for infected plants; remove them and protect remaining healthy plants through strict sanitation

Tomato___Healthy.description: Your tomato plant appears healthy with no visible signs of disease, pest damage, or nutritional deficiency. Healthy tomato plants have vibrant, deep green foliage, sturdy stems, and active growth. Continue your current care routine and monitor regularly to maintain plant health throughout the growing season.
symptoms: Vibrant, deep green leaves without spots, lesions, or discoloration
Sturdy stems with good turgor and upright posture
Active growth with normal internode spacing
Abundant, healthy flower and fruit set
No visible pest activity or webbing on leaves or stems
immediate_actions: Continue regular monitoring of plants at least twice a week for early signs of disease or pests
Maintain consistent watering, fertilization, and staking routines
Remove any yellowing lower leaves as a standard sanitation practice
Keep the area around plants free of weeds and fallen debris
chemical_treatment: No chemical treatment needed for healthy plants
Consider preventative applications of copper fungicides if disease pressure in the area is high
Avoid unnecessary pesticide applications to preserve beneficial insects
organic_treatment: Apply a balanced organic fertilizer to support continued vigorous growth
Use compost tea as a foliar spray to boost plant immunity and beneficial microorganisms
Apply neem oil preventatively every 2–3 weeks to deter pests and fungal spores
prevention: Water consistently at the base of plants using drip irrigation to keep foliage dry
Mulch around the base of plants to conserve moisture and prevent soil splash
Practice crop rotation annually to prevent soilborne disease buildup
Maintain proper plant nutrition with regular soil testing and balanced fertilization
Introduce and conserve beneficial insects (ladybugs, lacewings) for natural pest control
Ensure proper plant spacing for adequate air circulation throughout the canopy
Tomato___Healthy.recovery_time: N/A — plant is healthy; focus on maintaining current conditions
